metadata.description: Toxicity scoring dataset (GHS/CLP) extended to cover all the reagents, solvents and molecules of the two reaction datasets (reaction_dataset: 34 real synthesis routes; generic_reactions: 10,000 USPTO reactions). Methodology: score = sum (1 severity per independent hazard class, family rule) / 77. Tier A = harmonized classification Annex VI CLP; Tier B = vote C&L / concordant SDS sources; Tier C = non-classifiable (single organic substrates without CLP classification available, left null). The isolated ions of the generic ([Na+], [OH-], [BH4-]...) were recombined into real neutral salts (NaOH, NaBH4...) and then scored.
metadata.note: null where not authoritatively verifiable (not guessed).
metadata.scope_note: Complex organic substrates appearing only once in the generic (without harmonized CLP classification or C&L notification) are included in Tier C with hazard_score=null, in accordance with the treatment of bespoke intermediaries: they are not reliably classifiable and are not recurrent reagents/solvents.

tier_legend.A: Harmonized Classification Annex VI CLP (ECHA) — regulatory reference
tier_legend.B: Vote C&L / concordant supplier SDS sheets
tier_legend.C: Not classifiable and not predictable (left null)
tier_legend.P: PREDICTION by structural alerts QSAR (SMARTS) — NOT regulatory, NOT to be confused with A/B

metadata.prediction_disclaimer: The Tier P compounds are organic substrates without CLP classification. Their hazard_score comes from a structural alerts engine (functional groups SMARTS), not from a regulatory classification. A low score/0.0 means 'no structural alert triggered', which is NOT proof of safety (the model covers only ~19 known danger motifs and ignores structure-unrelated toxicity, e.g., benzene). The 'prediction_basis' field lists the alerts that contributed to the score. Do not use Tier P values for regulatory or real safety decisions.
